description.Transfer Eligibility Date: The date you are currently eligible for transfer to community supervision. This date may change based on your behavior, participation in work programs, Class assignment or program completion. If you have recently completed programming, this date might not yet have been updated, so please continue to check back regularly.
description.Parole Eligibility Date: The date you are currently eligible for transfer to community supervision. This date may change based on your behavior, participation in work programs, Class assignment or program completion. If you have recently completed programming, this date might not yet have been updated, so please continue to check back regularly.
description.Release Eligibility Date: The date you are currently eligible for transfer to community supervision. This date may change based on your behavior, participation in work programs, Class assignment or program completion. If you have recently completed programming, this date might not yet have been updated, so please continue to check back over the coming weeks.

maximumReleaseDate.label: Maximum Release Date
maximumReleaseDate.description: The latest date you can be held in custody, marking the full completion of your sentence.

moreInfo.heading: Understanding Release Dates & Time
moreInfo.body:
## Understanding Your Release Dates

Arkansas laws and policies have changed the names of release dates over the years. You can use the guide below to understand the specific terms used for your sentence.

### If Your Offense Was Committed Before 1994

<dl>
<dt>Parole Eligibility Date</dt>
<dd>The date you are currently eligible for transfer to community supervision. This date may change based on your behavior, participation in work programs, Class assignment or program completion. If you have recently completed programming, this date might not yet have been updated, so please continue to check back regularly.</dd>
<dt>Maximum Release Date</dt>
<dd>The latest date you can be held in custody, marking the full completion of your sentence.</dd>
<dt>Good Time</dt>
<dd>Days of credit that change your Parole Eligibility Date (PED) based on behavior, participation in work programs, Class level or program completion.</dd>
</dl>

### If Your Offense Was Committed Between 1994-2024

<dl>
<dt>Transfer Eligibility Date</dt>
<dd>The date you are currently eligible for transfer to community supervision. This date may change based on your behavior, participation in work programs, Class assignment or program completion. If you have recently completed programming, this date might not yet have been updated, so please continue to check back regularly.</dd>
<dt>Maximum Release Date</dt>
<dd>The latest date you can be held in custody, marking the full completion of your sentence.</dd>
<dt>Good Time</dt>
<dd>Days of credit that change your Transfer Eligibility Date (TED) based on behavior, participation in work programs, or Class level.</dd>
<dt>Earned Time</dt>
<dd>Days of credit that change your Transfer Eligibility Date (TED) based on successfully completing programming.</dd>
</dl>

#### Why does my time card say Parole Eligibility Date, but this app says Release Eligibility Date?

The term “Release Eligibility Date” is the updated term for when you become eligible to appear before the Parole Board (Post-Prison Transfer Board), based on the amount of earned time and good time you have accumulated.

## Good Time

### How many days do I reduce my sentence by for my Class level?

<section class="callout">
<dl>
<dt>Class I (one)</dt>
<dd>Your sentence is reduced by 30 days every month.</dd>
<dt>Class II (two)</dt>
<dd>Your sentence is reduced by 20 days every month.</dd>
<dt>Class III (three)</dt>
<dd>Your sentence is reduced by 10 days every month.</dd>
<dt>Class IV (four)</dt>
<dd>Your sentence is not reduced.</dd>
</dl>

</section>

### When do I not earn Good Time?

You will not earn Good Time if:

- You are in punitive
- You are at Class IV (four)
- You have a Life without Parole sentence

## Earned Time

If you are eligible, you can earn additional credit days by completing a state sponsored general education development certificate, a vocational program for which certification is awarded, or a drug or alcohol treatment program. For example, completing “Anger Management” earns you 20 days of credit.

### How do I know what programs are available for me?

To learn what programs are available for you, you can do any of the following:

- Check the kiosk if available
- Reach out to your unit’s program services

### I’m completing programs but my transfer/parole/release eligibility date isn’t moving. Why is that happening?

You have a limit on how much earned time you can receive through programming. If you are continuing to complete programs but have not seen your transfer/parole/release eligibility date move, you can ask the records office if you have hit your maximum for earned time.

moreInfo.heading: Understanding Release Dates & Time
moreInfo.body:
## Understanding Your Release Dates **If you committed an offense on or after January 1, 2025, then your sentence is governed by the Protect Arkansas Act.** **If this applies to you, then you may be able to leave prison via a transfer to post-release supervision earlier than your full sentence. The date that you can transfer is known as your "Release Eligibility Date." Here’s some guidance on factors that affect your Release Eligibility (RE):** By accruing **Earned Release Credits** (ERCs) through behavior, work, and program participation, you could shorten your time inside. There are two types of Earned Release Credits: **Programming** (earned from programs and approved activities) and **Work and Behavior** (earned based on your Class). How much time off you can get depends on your sentence type. **25% RE sentences:** - Up to 6 months of Earned Release Credits per year through programming - Up to 3 months of Earned Release Credits per year through work and behavior - You could reduce your time inside by **up to 9 months** for every year of your sentence **50% RE sentences:** - Up to 3 months of Earned Release Credits per year through programming - Up to 3 months of Earned Release Credits per year through work and behavior - You could reduce your time inside by **up to 6 months** for every year of your sentence **85% RE sentences:** - About 27 days of Earned Release Credits per year through programming - About 27 days of Earned Release Credits per year through work and behavior - You could reduce your time inside by about 54 days (**roughly 2 months**) for every year of your sentence **100% RE sentences:** - Some charges are not eligible for Earned Release Credits - If you are serving time on a 100% charge, the full sentence must be served in prison **Not sure which type of sentence you have?** Look at your sentencing paperwork known as a "Time Card" or ask the record staff for your unit. Your paperwork should say 25%, 50%, 85%, or 100%. When you first arrive at the Arkansas Department of Corrections your time card will show a Release Eligibility (RE) date that projects credit for **Work and Behavior**, but not for **Programming**. This means: - Your release date will get **earlier** as you complete programs - Your release date may get **pushed back or moved forward** based on your behavior ## Work and Behavior You can earn credit for **work and behavior** by following the rules, staying out of trouble, and getting approval on your application for a higher earning Class (for example Class I earns time off your sentence faster than Class II). The better your Class, the more you can earn: **up to 1 day off for every day you serve.**
Class I
1 day credit per day
Class II
1 day per 3 days
Class III
1 day per 7 days
Class IV
No credits
If you hit your maximum earned release credits from work and behavior, you will stop earning credit. For example, someone with a 25% sentence cannot earn more than 25% off their sentence from work and behavior. ## Programming You can earn credit by completing the **programs** listed in your case plan or other **approved activities** including: - Attending school - Completing your GED - Completing iCEV programs on your tablet - Completing mental health, substance abuse, and CTE programs - Participating in work release - Inmates in jail under Act 309 The programs in your case plan are matched to your specific needs based on an interview you completed when you first arrived (called the ORAS assessment) and are intended to prepare you for successful re-entry. How fast you earn programming credits is calculated based on: 1. Your ORAS assessment 2. The length of your sentence 3. The minimum percent of your sentence that you have to serve Each person’s credit earning rate is **specific to them.** Don’t try to compare yourself to others because their charges may be different. Their length of sentence may be different, their Class may be different, and/or their individual ORAS outcomes may be different. Each of these intentionally affects how credit earning works. It can be helpful to focus on understanding your situation rather than comparing yourself to others.
Here are two real examples to make it clearer
**Example 1:** Say you have a 5-year, 50% RE sentence. This would mean that you can earn 25% (15 months) of time from work and behavior and 25% (15 months) from programming. If your ORAS says you need 500 hours of programming, then your programming Earned Release Credits would reduce your time spent in confinement by 1.5 months for 50 hours of approved programming or equivalent.

**The math is:**
Total Possible Credit from Programming ÷ Total Programming Hours Required × 50 hours

15 months ÷ 500 hours × 50 hours = 1.5 months off for every 50 hours of activities completed.
**Example 2:** Say you have a 4-year, 25% RE sentence. This would mean that you can earn 25% (one year) of time from work and behavior and 50% (two years) from programming. If your ORAS says you need 1,000 hours of programming, then your programming Earned Release Credits would reduce your time spent in confinement by:

**The math is:**
24 months ÷ 1,000 hours × 50 hours = 1.2 months off for every 50 hours of activities completed.
If your ORAS assessment requires you to complete more programming, you will earn less time off your sentence for each 50-hour block because it will be a smaller share of the total programming you were assessed for.

programs.pageDescription:
This page helps you find programs and activities that can provide Good Time off of your Parole/Transfer/Release Eligibility Date. 

This list will update from time to time—look for the “New” label to see what was recently updated. If you are eligible for a particular program, please follow your facility’s process for signing up.
programs.learnMoreLink: Learn about Good Time and Programs
programs.modalCallToAction: Sound interesting? Reach out to your unit’s program services to see if this might be a good fit.
